document:
**Security Rule:** Every access decision must default to denial, granting access only when an explicit rule permits it, so any unmatched, unmapped, or new route or action is rejected rather than allowed.

**Vulnerability Pattern (Description):** The authorization logic permits access unless a rule explicitly forbids it, so a request that matches no rule, or a route added after the rules were written, is allowed through by accident.

**Secure Pattern (Description):** The authorization logic starts from a denied state and flips to allowed only when a matching grant is found, so anything not explicitly permitted is refused.

[[C# PATTERNS]]
Vulnerable:
```
var allowed = !Denied.Contains(action);
```
Secure:
```
var allowed = Permissions.TryGetValue(user.Role, out var p)
    && p.Contains(action);
```

[[GENERIC PATTERNS]]
Vulnerable:
```
allowed = true
for rule in denyRules: if rule.matches(req): allowed = false
```
Secure:
```
allowed = false
for rule in grantRules: if rule.matches(req): allowed = true
```

[[JAVA PATTERNS]]
Vulnerable:
```
boolean allowed = true;
if (DENIED.contains(action)) allowed = false;
```
Secure:
```
boolean allowed = permissions
    .getOrDefault(user.getRole(), Set.of()).contains(action);
```

[[JAVASCRIPT PATTERNS]]
Vulnerable:
```
function authorize(user, action) {
  return !deniedActions.includes(action);
}
```
Secure:
```
function authorize(user, action) {
  return (rolePermissions[user.role] || []).includes(action);
}
```

[[PYTHON PATTERNS]]
Vulnerable:
```
def can_access(user, action):
    if action in EXPLICITLY_DENIED:
        return False
    return True
```
Secure:
```
def can_access(user, action):
    return action in PERMISSIONS.get(user.role, set())
```
embed_text:
Every access decision must default to denial, granting access only when an explicit rule permits it, so any unmatched, unmapped, or new route or action is rejected rather than allowed.
The authorization logic permits access unless a rule explicitly forbids it, so a request that matches no rule, or a route added after the rules were written, is allowed through by accident.
The authorization logic starts from a denied state and flips to allowed only when a matching grant is found, so anything not explicitly permitted is refused.

**Security Rule:** Access control decisions must be made and enforced by trusted server-side code; client-side checks are only a usability aid and must never be the gate that protects a resource.

**Vulnerability Pattern (Description):** The server trusts a hidden form field, a request parameter, a cookie, or the mere absence of a UI element to convey a user's privileges, so a crafted request that the browser would never send bypasses the check entirely.

**Secure Pattern (Description):** The server re-derives the caller's identity and privileges from a trusted session or token and performs the authorization check itself on every request, ignoring any role or permission claimed by the client.

[[C# PATTERNS]]
Vulnerable:
```
if (Request.Form["role"] == "admin") DeleteUser(id);
```
Secure:
```
if (User.IsInRole("admin")) DeleteUser(id);
```

[[GENERIC PATTERNS]]
Vulnerable:
```
if request.param("isAdmin") == "true": deleteAccount(id)
```
Secure:
```
if session.user.role == "admin": deleteAccount(id)
```

[[JAVA PATTERNS]]
Vulnerable:
```
if ("admin".equals(request.getParameter("role"))) deleteUser(id);
```
Secure:
```
if (authService.currentUser().hasRole("admin")) deleteUser(id);
```

[[JAVASCRIPT PATTERNS]]
Vulnerable:
```
if (req.body.role === "admin") deleteUser(req.body.targetId);
```
Secure:
```
if (req.user.role === "admin") deleteUser(req.body.targetId);
```

[[PYTHON PATTERNS]]
Vulnerable:
```
if request.form.get("role") == "admin":
    delete_user(target_id)
```
Secure:
```
if current_user.is_admin:
    delete_user(target_id)
```
embed_text:
Access control decisions must be made and enforced by trusted server-side code; client-side checks are only a usability aid and must never be the gate that protects a resource.
The server trusts a hidden form field, a request parameter, a cookie, or the mere absence of a UI element to convey a user's privileges, so a crafted request that the browser would never send bypasses the check entirely.
The server re-derives the caller's identity and privileges from a trusted session or token and performs the authorization check itself on every request, ignoring any role or permission claimed by the client.

document:
**Security Rule:** When a request names a specific record by an identifier, the server must verify the authenticated caller owns or is otherwise entitled to that record before acting, preventing insecure direct object references.

**Vulnerability Pattern (Description):** A record is looked up by an identifier taken straight from the request and returned or modified without checking who owns it, so any user can read or change another user's data by changing the id.

**Secure Pattern (Description):** The lookup is constrained to records the caller is entitled to — typically by adding the caller's user id to the query predicate — so a request for someone else's record returns nothing.

[[C# PATTERNS]]
Vulnerable:
```
var file = _db.Files.Find(id);
```
Secure:
```
var file = _db.Files.FirstOrDefault(
    f => f.Id == id && f.OwnerId == currentUser.Id);
```

[[GENERIC PATTERNS]]
Vulnerable:
```
record = db.find(Invoice, request.param("id"))
```
Secure:
```
record = db.find(Invoice, id=request.param("id"),
                  owner_id=session.user.id)
```

[[JAVA PATTERNS]]
Vulnerable:
```
Document doc = repo.findById(id).orElseThrow();
```
Secure:
```
Document doc = repo.findByIdAndOwnerId(id, currentUser.getId())
    .orElseThrow(ForbiddenException::new);
```

[[JAVASCRIPT PATTERNS]]
Vulnerable:
```
const order = await Order.findById(req.params.id);
```
Secure:
```
const order = await Order.findOne({
  _id: req.params.id, ownerId: req.user.id });
```

[[PYTHON PATTERNS]]
Vulnerable:
```
invoice = Invoice.query.get(request.args["id"])
```
Secure:
```
invoice = Invoice.query.filter_by(
    id=request.args["id"], owner_id=current_user.id).first_or_404()
```
embed_text:
When a request names a specific record by an identifier, the server must verify the authenticated caller owns or is otherwise entitled to that record before acting, preventing insecure direct object references.
A record is looked up by an identifier taken straight from the request and returned or modified without checking who owns it, so any user can read or change another user's data by changing the id.
The lookup is constrained to records the caller is entitled to — typically by adding the caller's user id to the query predicate — so a request for someone else's record returns nothing.

**Security Rule:** Every user, service account, token, and process must be granted only the minimum permissions needed for its task, and elevated rights must be scoped narrowly and revoked when no longer required.

**Vulnerability Pattern (Description):** A component runs with broad rights — a shared admin database account, a wildcard cloud policy, or a token with full scope — so any flaw in that component immediately exposes far more than it ever needed.

**Secure Pattern (Description):** Each component is given a dedicated identity scoped to exactly the actions and resources it uses, so a compromise is contained to that narrow surface.

[[C# PATTERNS]]
Vulnerable:
```
var conn = new SqlConnection("Server=db;User Id=sa;Password=pw;");
```
Secure:
```
var conn = new SqlConnection("Server=db;User Id=reports_reader;Password=pw;");
```

[[GENERIC PATTERNS]]
Vulnerable:
```
connect(db, user="root", password=secret)   # full DBA rights
```
Secure:
```
connect(db, user="orders_svc", password=secret)  # SELECT/INSERT on orders
```

[[JAVA PATTERNS]]
Vulnerable:
```
var policy = AwsPolicy.allow("s3:*", "*");
```
Secure:
```
var policy = AwsPolicy.allow("s3:GetObject", "arn:aws:s3:::reports/*");
```

[[JAVASCRIPT PATTERNS]]
Vulnerable:
```
const token = jwt.sign({ sub: id, scope: "*" }, key);
```
Secure:
```
const token = jwt.sign({ sub: id, scope: "orders:read" }, key);
```

[[PYTHON PATTERNS]]
Vulnerable:
```
engine = create_engine("postgresql://admin:pw@db/app")
```
Secure:
```
engine = create_engine("postgresql://orders_ro:pw@db/app")  # read-only role
```
embed_text:
Every user, service account, token, and process must be granted only the minimum permissions needed for its task, and elevated rights must be scoped narrowly and revoked when no longer required.
A component runs with broad rights — a shared admin database account, a wildcard cloud policy, or a token with full scope — so any flaw in that component immediately exposes far more than it ever needed.
Each component is given a dedicated identity scoped to exactly the actions and resources it uses, so a compromise is contained to that narrow surface.

Authorization decisions must flow through a single, well-tested component or policy layer rather than being re-implemented inline at each call site, so the rules stay consistent and auditable.
Each endpoint hand-codes its own role and permission checks, so the logic drifts between handlers, a new endpoint silently ships with no check, and a rule change has to be found and applied in many places.
A shared authorization service, decorator, middleware, or policy object evaluates every access decision against one rule set, so all endpoints enforce the same logic and an added endpoint inherits it.

When an authorization check throws an error, times out, or returns an indeterminate result, the system must treat that outcome as a denial rather than allowing the request to proceed.
An exception in the permission check is swallowed, or a catch block falls through, leaving execution to continue past the gate as though access had been granted.
Any error or ambiguous result from the authorization path is caught and converted into an explicit denial before the protected operation is reached.

document:
**Security Rule:** Authorization must be enforced on every state-changing and data-returning function — including APIs, background actions, and administrative routes — not only on the functions reachable from the navigation a normal user sees.

**Vulnerability Pattern (Description):** A sensitive function is protected only because the UI hides its link, while the underlying route, API endpoint, or method has no check, so a user who guesses or forces the URL reaches it directly.

**Secure Pattern (Description):** Each sensitive function carries its own server-side authorization check independent of the UI, so reaching it by any path still requires the appropriate permission.

[[C# PATTERNS]]
Vulnerable:
```
[HttpGet("admin/report")]
public Report Report() => _reports.Build();
```
Secure:
```
[Authorize(Roles = "Admin")]
[HttpGet("admin/report")]
public Report Report() => _reports.Build();
```

[[GENERIC PATTERNS]]
Vulnerable:
```
# /admin/export has no check — only hidden from the menu
def admin_export(): return dump_all_users()
```
Secure:
```
@requires_role("admin")
def admin_export(): return dump_all_users()
```

[[JAVA PATTERNS]]
Vulnerable:
```
@GetMapping("/admin/report")
public Report report() { return reportService.build(); }
```
Secure:
```
@PreAuthorize("hasRole('ADMIN')")
@GetMapping("/admin/report")
public Report report() { return reportService.build(); }
```

[[JAVASCRIPT PATTERNS]]
Vulnerable:
```
router.get("/admin/metrics", (req, res) => res.json(getMetrics()));
```
Secure:
```
router.get("/admin/metrics", requireRole("admin"),
           (req, res) => res.json(getMetrics()));
```

[[PYTHON PATTERNS]]
Vulnerable:
```
@app.get("/api/admin/users")
def list_users():
    return User.query.all()
```
Secure:
```
@app.get("/api/admin/users")
def list_users(_=Depends(require_role("admin"))):
    return User.query.all()
```
embed_text:
Authorization must be enforced on every state-changing and data-returning function — including APIs, background actions, and administrative routes — not only on the functions reachable from the navigation a normal user sees.
A sensitive function is protected only because the UI hides its link, while the underlying route, API endpoint, or method has no check, so a user who guesses or forces the URL reaches it directly.
Each sensitive function carries its own server-side authorization check independent of the UI, so reaching it by any path still requires the appropriate permission.

**Security Rule:** When a decision depends on context — the resource's attributes, the relationship between caller and resource, time, or location — use an attribute or relationship based model rather than coarse static roles that cannot express the rule.

**Vulnerability Pattern (Description):** A coarse role check stands in for a contextual rule, so a user with the right role can act on resources outside their team, region, or relationship because the role alone cannot capture the constraint.

**Secure Pattern (Description):** The decision evaluates the caller's attributes against the resource's attributes — for example matching department, tenant, or assignment — so permission depends on the actual relationship, not just the role.

[[C# PATTERNS]]
Vulnerable:
```
if (user.Role == "Manager") Approve(expense);
```
Secure:
```
if (user.Role == "Manager" && user.TenantId == expense.TenantId)
    Approve(expense);
```

[[GENERIC PATTERNS]]
Vulnerable:
```
if user.role == "manager": approve(expense)
```
Secure:
```
if user.role == "manager" and user.dept == expense.dept:
    approve(expense)
```

[[JAVA PATTERNS]]
Vulnerable:
```
if (user.hasRole("MANAGER")) approve(expense);
```
Secure:
```
if (user.hasRole("MANAGER")
        && user.getDeptId().equals(expense.getDeptId()))
    approve(expense);
```

[[JAVASCRIPT PATTERNS]]
Vulnerable:
```
if (user.role === "manager") approveExpense(expense);
```
Secure:
```
if (user.role === "manager" && user.teamId === expense.teamId)
  approveExpense(expense);
```

[[PYTHON PATTERNS]]
Vulnerable:
```
if current_user.role == "manager":
    approve(expense)
```
Secure:
```
if current_user.role == "manager" \
        and current_user.department_id == expense.department_id:
    approve(expense)
```
embed_text:
When a decision depends on context — the resource's attributes, the relationship between caller and resource, time, or location — use an attribute or relationship based model rather than coarse static roles that cannot express the rule.
A coarse role check stands in for a contextual rule, so a user with the right role can act on resources outside their team, region, or relationship because the role alone cannot capture the constraint.
The decision evaluates the caller's attributes against the resource's attributes — for example matching department, tenant, or assignment — so permission depends on the actual relationship, not just the role.

Failed authorization attempts must be logged with the caller identity, the resource, and the action so that probing, privilege escalation attempts, and broken-access-control abuse are detectable.
A denied request is rejected silently with only an error response, so a user systematically enumerating resources or roles leaves no trail for monitoring or alerting to act on.
Every authorization denial emits a structured security log event recording who was denied, what they tried to reach, and the action, feeding detection and alerting.

**Security Rule:** Encrypting data must use an authenticated encryption mode that provides both confidentiality and integrity, so ciphertext tampering is detected on decryption rather than silently accepted.

**Vulnerability Pattern (Description):** Data is encrypted with an unauthenticated mode such as ECB or raw CBC, so the ciphertext carries no integrity tag and an attacker can flip bits or reorder blocks without detection.

**Secure Pattern (Description):** An authenticated encryption mode such as AES-GCM or ChaCha20-Poly1305 is used, producing an authentication tag that decryption verifies before returning any plaintext.

[[C# PATTERNS]]
Vulnerable:
```
using var aes = Aes.Create();   // CBC, no integrity tag
```
Secure:
```
using var gcm = new AesGcm(key, 16);
gcm.Encrypt(nonce, plaintext, ciphertext, tag);
```

[[GENERIC PATTERNS]]
Vulnerable:
```
ciphertext = aesEncrypt(key, plaintext, mode="CBC")
```
Secure:
```
ciphertext, tag = aesGcmEncrypt(key, nonce, plaintext)
```

[[JAVA PATTERNS]]
Vulnerable:
```
Cipher c = Cipher.getInstance("AES/CBC/PKCS5Padding");
```
Secure:
```
Cipher c = Cipher.getInstance("AES/GCM/NoPadding");
c.init(Cipher.ENCRYPT_MODE, key, new GCMParameterSpec(128, nonce));
```

[[JAVASCRIPT PATTERNS]]
Vulnerable:
```
const c = crypto.createCipheriv("aes-256-cbc", key, iv);
```
Secure:
```
const c = crypto.createCipheriv("aes-256-gcm", key, nonce);
const ct = Buffer.concat([c.update(pt), c.final(), c.getAuthTag()]);
```

[[PYTHON PATTERNS]]
Vulnerable:
```
cipher = Cipher(algorithms.AES(key), modes.CBC(iv)).encryptor()
```
Secure:
```
ct = AESGCM(key).encrypt(nonce, plaintext, associated_data=None)
```
embed_text:
Encrypting data must use an authenticated encryption mode that provides both confidentiality and integrity, so ciphertext tampering is detected on decryption rather than silently accepted.
Data is encrypted with an unauthenticated mode such as ECB or raw CBC, so the ciphertext carries no integrity tag and an attacker can flip bits or reorder blocks without detection.
An authenticated encryption mode such as AES-GCM or ChaCha20-Poly1305 is used, producing an authentication tag that decryption verifies before returning any plaintext.

**Security Rule:** Cryptographic operations must use current, standards-approved algorithms and key sizes, and must not use algorithms that are broken or deprecated such as DES, RC4, or MD5.

**Vulnerability Pattern (Description):** A weak or obsolete cipher or hash such as DES, 3DES, RC4, or MD5 is used, so the protection it provides can be defeated with modern computing resources or known cryptanalysis.

**Secure Pattern (Description):** A strong modern primitive is selected — AES-256 or ChaCha20 for encryption and SHA-256 or SHA-3 for hashing — with key sizes that meet current guidance.

[[C# PATTERNS]]
Vulnerable:
```
using var md = MD5.Create();
```
Secure:
```
using var sha = SHA256.Create();
```

[[GENERIC PATTERNS]]
Vulnerable:
```
digest = md5(data)
```
Secure:
```
digest = sha256(data)
```

[[JAVA PATTERNS]]
Vulnerable:
```
MessageDigest md = MessageDigest.getInstance("MD5");
```
Secure:
```
MessageDigest md = MessageDigest.getInstance("SHA-256");
```

[[JAVASCRIPT PATTERNS]]
Vulnerable:
```
const h = crypto.createHash("md5").update(data).digest("hex");
```
Secure:
```
const h = crypto.createHash("sha256").update(data).digest("hex");
```

[[PYTHON PATTERNS]]
Vulnerable:
```
h = hashlib.md5(data).hexdigest()
```
Secure:
```
h = hashlib.sha256(data).hexdigest()
```
embed_text:
Cryptographic operations must use current, standards-approved algorithms and key sizes, and must not use algorithms that are broken or deprecated such as DES, RC4, or MD5.
A weak or obsolete cipher or hash such as DES, 3DES, RC4, or MD5 is used, so the protection it provides can be defeated with modern computing resources or known cryptanalysis.
A strong modern primitive is selected — AES-256 or ChaCha20 for encryption and SHA-256 or SHA-3 for hashing — with key sizes that meet current guidance.

**Security Rule:** Values that must be unpredictable — tokens, session identifiers, nonces, salts, and keys — must be generated with a cryptographically secure random number generator.

**Vulnerability Pattern (Description):** A general-purpose or statistical pseudo-random generator is used to produce a security-sensitive value, so its output is predictable and an attacker can guess or reproduce the value.

**Secure Pattern (Description):** A cryptographically secure random source is used for every security-sensitive value, drawing from the operating system entropy pool with sufficient length.

[[C# PATTERNS]]
Vulnerable:
```
var token = new Random().Next();
```
Secure:
```
var token = RandomNumberGenerator.GetBytes(32);
```

[[GENERIC PATTERNS]]
Vulnerable:
```
token = random()
```
Secure:
```
token = secureRandomBytes(32)
```

[[JAVA PATTERNS]]
Vulnerable:
```
long token = new Random().nextLong();
```
Secure:
```
byte[] token = new byte[32];
SecureRandom.getInstanceStrong().nextBytes(token);
```

[[JAVASCRIPT PATTERNS]]
Vulnerable:
```
const token = Math.random().toString(36).slice(2);
```
Secure:
```
const token = crypto.randomBytes(32).toString("hex");
```

[[PYTHON PATTERNS]]
Vulnerable:
```
token = "".join(random.choice(chars) for _ in range(32))
```
Secure:
```
token = secrets.token_urlsafe(32)
```
embed_text:
Values that must be unpredictable — tokens, session identifiers, nonces, salts, and keys — must be generated with a cryptographically secure random number generator.
A general-purpose or statistical pseudo-random generator is used to produce a security-sensitive value, so its output is predictable and an attacker can guess or reproduce the value.
A cryptographically secure random source is used for every security-sensitive value, drawing from the operating system entropy pool with sufficient length.

**Security Rule:** Stored user passwords must be protected with a memory-hard, salted, adaptive password hashing function such as Argon2id, scrypt, or bcrypt, never with a plain or fast cryptographic hash.

**Vulnerability Pattern (Description):** Passwords are stored with a fast unsalted hash such as SHA-256 or MD5, so an attacker who obtains the database can crack the hashes at high speed with rainbow tables or brute force.

**Secure Pattern (Description):** Passwords are hashed with a dedicated adaptive function that applies a unique per-password salt and a tunable work factor, making offline cracking deliberately expensive.

[[C# PATTERNS]]
Vulnerable:
```
var stored = Convert.ToHexString(SHA256.HashData(bytes));
```
Secure:
```
var stored = BCrypt.Net.BCrypt.HashPassword(password, workFactor: 12);
```

[[GENERIC PATTERNS]]
Vulnerable:
```
stored = sha256(password)
```
Secure:
```
stored = argon2idHash(password)
```

[[JAVA PATTERNS]]
Vulnerable:
```
String stored = sha256Hex(password);
```
Secure:
```
String stored = new Argon2PasswordEncoder(16, 32, 1, 19456, 2).encode(password);
```

[[JAVASCRIPT PATTERNS]]
Vulnerable:
```
const stored = crypto.createHash("sha256").update(password).digest("hex");
```
Secure:
```
const stored = await argon2.hash(password, { type: argon2.argon2id });
```

[[PYTHON PATTERNS]]
Vulnerable:
```
stored = hashlib.sha256(password.encode()).hexdigest()
```
Secure:
```
from argon2 import PasswordHasher
stored = PasswordHasher().hash(password)
```
embed_text:
Stored user passwords must be protected with a memory-hard, salted, adaptive password hashing function such as Argon2id, scrypt, or bcrypt, never with a plain or fast cryptographic hash.
Passwords are stored with a fast unsalted hash such as SHA-256 or MD5, so an attacker who obtains the database can crack the hashes at high speed with rainbow tables or brute force.
Passwords are hashed with a dedicated adaptive function that applies a unique per-password salt and a tunable work factor, making offline cracking deliberately expensive.

**Security Rule:** Cryptographic keys must not be hardcoded in source code, and must be loaded from a secrets manager, key vault, or protected environment configuration that supports rotation.

**Vulnerability Pattern (Description):** An encryption key, signing key, or other secret is embedded as a literal in the source code, so anyone with read access to the repository can decrypt or forge protected data.

**Secure Pattern (Description):** Keys are stored in a dedicated secrets manager or key management service and fetched at runtime, keeping them out of source control and allowing rotation without a code change.

[[C# PATTERNS]]
Vulnerable:
```
const string Key = "0123456789abcdef0123456789abcdef";
```
Secure:
```
var key = await keyVaultClient.GetSecretAsync("encryption-key");
```

[[GENERIC PATTERNS]]
Vulnerable:
```
key = "5f4dcc3b5aa765d61d8327deb882cf99"
```
Secure:
```
key = secretsManager.getSecret("data-encryption-key")
```

[[JAVA PATTERNS]]
Vulnerable:
```
String key = "AKIA-hardcoded-secret-key-value";
```
Secure:
```
String key = secretsClient.getSecretValue(req).secretString();
```

[[JAVASCRIPT PATTERNS]]
Vulnerable:
```
const key = "supersecretkey1234567890abcdef12";
```
Secure:
```
const key = await vault.read("secret/data/encryption-key");
```

[[PYTHON PATTERNS]]
Vulnerable:
```
KEY = b"hardcoded-aes-key-do-not-ship!!"
```
Secure:
```
KEY = secrets_client.get_secret_value(SecretId="data-key")["SecretBinary"]
```
embed_text:
Cryptographic keys must not be hardcoded in source code, and must be loaded from a secrets manager, key vault, or protected environment configuration that supports rotation.
An encryption key, signing key, or other secret is embedded as a literal in the source code, so anyone with read access to the repository can decrypt or forge protected data.
Keys are stored in a dedicated secrets manager or key management service and fetched at runtime, keeping them out of source control and allowing rotation without a code change.

document:
**Security Rule:** Sensitive data must be transmitted over connections protected with current TLS, and connections must verify the server certificate and hostname rather than disabling validation.

**Vulnerability Pattern (Description):** Data is sent over plaintext HTTP, or a TLS client disables certificate or hostname verification, so a network attacker can intercept or alter the traffic in a machine-in-the-middle position.

**Secure Pattern (Description):** Connections use HTTPS with a modern TLS version, and certificate and hostname validation are left enabled so the peer identity is confirmed before any sensitive data is exchanged.

[[C# PATTERNS]]
Vulnerable:
```
handler.ServerCertificateCustomValidationCallback = (m, c, ch, e) => true;
```
Secure:
```
var handler = new HttpClientHandler();   // default certificate validation
```

[[GENERIC PATTERNS]]
Vulnerable:
```
response = httpGet("http://api.internal/data")
```
Secure:
```
response = httpsGet("https://api.internal/data", verifyTls=true)
```

[[JAVA PATTERNS]]
Vulnerable:
```
sslContext.init(null, new TrustManager[]{ trustAllCerts }, null);
```
Secure:
```
SSLContext ctx = SSLContext.getDefault();   // platform trust + hostname check
```

[[JAVASCRIPT PATTERNS]]
Vulnerable:
```
https.get(url, { rejectUnauthorized: false }, cb);
```
Secure:
```
https.get(url, { rejectUnauthorized: true }, cb);
```

[[PYTHON PATTERNS]]
Vulnerable:
```
r = requests.get(url, verify=False)
```
Secure:
```
r = requests.get(url, verify=True)
```
embed_text:
Sensitive data must be transmitted over connections protected with current TLS, and connections must verify the server certificate and hostname rather than disabling validation.
Data is sent over plaintext HTTP, or a TLS client disables certificate or hostname verification, so a network attacker can intercept or alter the traffic in a machine-in-the-middle position.
Connections use HTTPS with a modern TLS version, and certificate and hostname validation are left enabled so the peer identity is confirmed before any sensitive data is exchanged.

document:
**Security Rule:** Sensitive data such as credentials, tokens, and personal information must be encrypted before it is written to persistent storage, not saved in plaintext.

**Vulnerability Pattern (Description):** Sensitive fields are persisted to a database, file, or log in plaintext, so anyone who reads the storage medium — including from a backup or a stolen disk — sees the raw values.

**Secure Pattern (Description):** Sensitive values are encrypted with an authenticated cipher and a managed key before being stored, so the data on disk is unreadable without access to the key.

[[C# PATTERNS]]
Vulnerable:
```
entity.AccessToken = token;
```
Secure:
```
entity.AccessToken = dataProtector.Protect(token);
```

[[GENERIC PATTERNS]]
Vulnerable:
```
db.save(user_id, apiToken)
```
Secure:
```
db.save(user_id, encrypt(key, apiToken))
```

[[JAVA PATTERNS]]
Vulnerable:
```
statement.setString(1, accountSecret);
```
Secure:
```
statement.setBytes(1, aesGcm.encrypt(accountSecret.getBytes()));
```

[[JAVASCRIPT PATTERNS]]
Vulnerable:
```
await db.users.update({ id }, { cardNumber });
```
Secure:
```
await db.users.update({ id }, { cardNumber: encryptField(key, cardNumber) });
```

[[PYTHON PATTERNS]]
Vulnerable:
```
record.ssn = ssn
```
Secure:
```
record.ssn = Fernet(key).encrypt(ssn.encode())
```
embed_text:
Sensitive data such as credentials, tokens, and personal information must be encrypted before it is written to persistent storage, not saved in plaintext.
Sensitive fields are persisted to a database, file, or log in plaintext, so anyone who reads the storage medium — including from a backup or a stolen disk — sees the raw values.
Sensitive values are encrypted with an authenticated cipher and a managed key before being stored, so the data on disk is unreadable without access to the key.

**Security Rule:** Every encryption operation must use a unique initialization vector or nonce, generated fresh per message and never reused under the same key.

**Vulnerability Pattern (Description):** A fixed, zero, or reused initialization vector is supplied to the cipher, so identical plaintexts produce identical ciphertext and, for counter-based modes, the keystream is catastrophically reused.

**Secure Pattern (Description):** A fresh random or strictly counter-incremented nonce of the correct length is generated for each encryption call and stored or transmitted alongside the ciphertext.

[[C# PATTERNS]]
Vulnerable:
```
var nonce = new byte[12];   // all zeros, reused every call
```
Secure:
```
var nonce = RandomNumberGenerator.GetBytes(12);
```

[[GENERIC PATTERNS]]
Vulnerable:
```
ciphertext = encrypt(key, plaintext, iv=STATIC_IV)
```
Secure:
```
iv = secureRandomBytes(12)
ciphertext = encrypt(key, plaintext, iv=iv)
```

[[JAVA PATTERNS]]
Vulnerable:
```
var spec = new GCMParameterSpec(128, new byte[12]);
```
Secure:
```
byte[] iv = new byte[12];
SecureRandom.getInstanceStrong().nextBytes(iv);
var spec = new GCMParameterSpec(128, iv);
```

[[JAVASCRIPT PATTERNS]]
Vulnerable:
```
const c = crypto.createCipheriv("aes-256-gcm", key, ZERO_IV);
```
Secure:
```
const iv = crypto.randomBytes(12);
const c = crypto.createCipheriv("aes-256-gcm", key, iv);
```

[[PYTHON PATTERNS]]
Vulnerable:
```
ct = AESGCM(key).encrypt(b"000000000000", plaintext, None)
```
Secure:
```
nonce = os.urandom(12)
ct = AESGCM(key).encrypt(nonce, plaintext, None)
```
embed_text:
Every encryption operation must use a unique initialization vector or nonce, generated fresh per message and never reused under the same key.
A fixed, zero, or reused initialization vector is supplied to the cipher, so identical plaintexts produce identical ciphertext and, for counter-based modes, the keystream is catastrophically reused.
A fresh random or strictly counter-incremented nonce of the correct length is generated for each encryption call and stored or transmitted alongside the ciphertext.

document:
**Security Rule:** Secret values such as authentication tokens, signatures, and message authentication codes must be compared with a constant-time routine so comparison timing does not leak how much matched.

**Vulnerability Pattern (Description):** A secret is compared with an ordinary equality check that returns as soon as the first differing byte is found, so an attacker can measure response timing to recover the secret one byte at a time.

**Secure Pattern (Description):** Secret comparisons use a constant-time equality function that always examines every byte, so the running time reveals nothing about how many leading bytes matched.

[[C# PATTERNS]]
Vulnerable:
```
if (providedToken == expectedToken) Accept();
```
Secure:
```
if (CryptographicOperations.FixedTimeEquals(provided, expected)) Accept();
```

[[GENERIC PATTERNS]]
Vulnerable:
```
if providedToken == expectedToken: grantAccess()
```
Secure:
```
if constantTimeEquals(providedToken, expectedToken): grantAccess()
```

[[JAVA PATTERNS]]
Vulnerable:
```
if (providedToken.equals(expectedToken)) accept();
```
Secure:
```
if (MessageDigest.isEqual(providedBytes, expectedBytes)) accept();
```

[[JAVASCRIPT PATTERNS]]
Vulnerable:
```
if (providedSig === expectedSig) accept();
```
Secure:
```
if (crypto.timingSafeEqual(Buffer.from(providedSig), Buffer.from(expectedSig)))
  accept();
```

[[PYTHON PATTERNS]]
Vulnerable:
```
if provided_mac == expected_mac:
    accept()
```
Secure:
```
if hmac.compare_digest(provided_mac, expected_mac):
    accept()
```
embed_text:
Secret values such as authentication tokens, signatures, and message authentication codes must be compared with a constant-time routine so comparison timing does not leak how much matched.
A secret is compared with an ordinary equality check that returns as soon as the first differing byte is found, so an attacker can measure response timing to recover the secret one byte at a time.
Secret comparisons use a constant-time equality function that always examines every byte, so the running time reveals nothing about how many leading bytes matched.

document:
**Security Rule:** Teams must classify which data requires cryptographic protection and maintain an inventory of the algorithms, key sizes, and key locations in use so weak or expired cryptography can be found and replaced.

**Vulnerability Pattern (Description):** No classification of sensitive data or inventory of cryptographic use exists, so weak algorithms, undersized keys, and expired keys persist unnoticed and protection is applied inconsistently.

**Secure Pattern (Description):** Sensitive data is classified by protection need, and a maintained cryptographic inventory records each algorithm, key size, key owner, and rotation date, enabling planned upgrades and crypto-agility.

[[GENERIC PATTERNS]]
Vulnerable:
```
# no data classification; crypto choices scattered and untracked
encryptSomewhere(data)
```
Secure:
```
# classification + inventory drive a single reviewed crypto policy
cryptoPolicy.encrypt(classify(data), data)
```
embed_text:
Teams must classify which data requires cryptographic protection and maintain an inventory of the algorithms, key sizes, and key locations in use so weak or expired cryptography can be found and replaced.
No classification of sensitive data or inventory of cryptographic use exists, so weak algorithms, undersized keys, and expired keys persist unnoticed and protection is applied inconsistently.
Sensitive data is classified by protection need, and a maintained cryptographic inventory records each algorithm, key size, key owner, and rotation date, enabling planned upgrades and crypto-agility.

**Security Rule:** All input that crosses a trust boundary, including request bodies, headers, query parameters, and inter-service messages, must be validated on the server before it is used, regardless of any client-side checks.

**Vulnerability Pattern (Description):** Input is accepted and used directly because validation was only performed in the browser or trusted because it came from another internal service, so a crafted request that skips the client reaches the sink unchecked.

**Secure Pattern (Description):** Every value is re-validated server-side at the boundary where it enters the application, treating client-side validation purely as a usability aid and never as a security control.

[[C# PATTERNS]]
Vulnerable:
```
repo.Save(Request.Form["age"]);
```
Secure:
```
repo.Save(InputValidator.RequireAge(Request.Form["age"]));
```

[[GENERIC PATTERNS]]
Vulnerable:
```
save(request.body)            # client-side form already validated
```
Secure:
```
data = validateOnServer(request.body)
save(data)
```

[[JAVA PATTERNS]]
Vulnerable:
```
service.update(request.getParameter("age"));
```
Secure:
```
service.update(InputValidator.requireAge(request.getParameter("age")));
```

[[JAVASCRIPT PATTERNS]]
Vulnerable:
```
app.post("/u", (req, res) => db.save(req.body));
```
Secure:
```
app.post("/u", (req, res) => db.save(userSchema.parse(req.body)));
```

[[PYTHON PATTERNS]]
Vulnerable:
```
age = int(request.form["age"])      # trusts the HTML5 number input
```
Secure:
```
age = validate_age(request.form.get("age"))   # server-side check
```
embed_text:
All input that crosses a trust boundary, including request bodies, headers, query parameters, and inter-service messages, must be validated on the server before it is used, regardless of any client-side checks.
Input is accepted and used directly because validation was only performed in the browser or trusted because it came from another internal service, so a crafted request that skips the client reaches the sink unchecked.
Every value is re-validated server-side at the boundary where it enters the application, treating client-side validation purely as a usability aid and never as a security control.

document:
**Security Rule:** Each input must be checked for correct syntax — its data type, length, numeric range, and character set — so that structurally malformed values are rejected before any processing.

**Vulnerability Pattern (Description):** A value is consumed without checking its type, length, or format, so an oversized string, a non-numeric value, or an out-of-range number triggers errors, resource exhaustion, or unexpected behaviour downstream.

**Secure Pattern (Description):** The value is verified to match the expected type, to fall within a bounded length and numeric range, and to contain only the expected characters before it is used.

[[C# PATTERNS]]
Vulnerable:
```
var name = Request.Form["username"].ToString();
```
Secure:
```
var name = Request.Form["username"].ToString();
if (!Regex.IsMatch(name, "^[A-Za-z0-9_]{3,32}$")) return BadRequest();
```

[[GENERIC PATTERNS]]
Vulnerable:
```
quantity = toNumber(input)
```
Secure:
```
quantity = toNumber(input)
if not isInteger(quantity) or quantity < 1 or quantity > 999: reject()
```

[[JAVA PATTERNS]]
Vulnerable:
```
String name = request.getParameter("username");
```
Secure:
```
String name = request.getParameter("username");
if (name == null || !name.matches("[A-Za-z0-9_]{3,32}")) throw new BadRequest();
```

[[JAVASCRIPT PATTERNS]]
Vulnerable:
```
const qty = req.body.qty;
```
Secure:
```
const qty = Number(req.body.qty);
if (!Number.isInteger(qty) || qty < 1 || qty > 999) return res.sendStatus(400);
```

[[PYTHON PATTERNS]]
Vulnerable:
```
username = request.form["username"]
```
Secure:
```
username = request.form["username"]
if not re.fullmatch(r"[A-Za-z0-9_]{3,32}", username): abort(400)
```
embed_text:
Each input must be checked for correct syntax — its data type, length, numeric range, and character set — so that structurally malformed values are rejected before any processing.
A value is consumed without checking its type, length, or format, so an oversized string, a non-numeric value, or an out-of-range number triggers errors, resource exhaustion, or unexpected behaviour downstream.
The value is verified to match the expected type, to fall within a bounded length and numeric range, and to contain only the expected characters before it is used.

**Security Rule:** Validation must define what is acceptable with an allowlist of known good values or patterns, rather than attempting to enumerate and block known bad values with a denylist.

**Vulnerability Pattern (Description):** Validation tries to strip or reject a fixed set of dangerous tokens, so any malicious variant the denylist did not anticipate, including encoded or novel forms, passes straight through.

**Secure Pattern (Description):** The input is compared against an explicit allowlist of permitted values or a strict pattern of permitted characters, and anything not on the allowlist is rejected by default.

[[C# PATTERNS]]
Vulnerable:
```
if (sortField.Contains(";")) return BadRequest();
```
Secure:
```
var allowed = new[] { "name", "createdAt", "price" };
if (!allowed.Contains(sortField)) return BadRequest();
```

[[GENERIC PATTERNS]]
Vulnerable:
```
if input contains "../" or input contains "<": reject()
```
Secure:
```
if input not in ALLOWED_VALUES: reject()
```

[[JAVA PATTERNS]]
Vulnerable:
```
if (sortField.contains(";")) throw new BadRequest();
```
Secure:
```
if (!Set.of("name", "createdAt", "price").contains(sortField))
    throw new BadRequest();
```

[[JAVASCRIPT PATTERNS]]
Vulnerable:
```
if (/script/i.test(color)) return res.sendStatus(400);
```
Secure:
```
if (!["red", "green", "blue"].includes(color)) return res.sendStatus(400);
```

[[PYTHON PATTERNS]]
Vulnerable:
```
if "DROP" in sort_field.upper(): abort(400)
```
Secure:
```
if sort_field not in {"name", "created_at", "price"}: abort(400)
```
embed_text:
Validation must define what is acceptable with an allowlist of known good values or patterns, rather than attempting to enumerate and block known bad values with a denylist.
Validation tries to strip or reject a fixed set of dangerous tokens, so any malicious variant the denylist did not anticipate, including encoded or novel forms, passes straight through.
The input is compared against an explicit allowlist of permitted values or a strict pattern of permitted characters, and anything not on the allowlist is rejected by default.

**Security Rule:** Beyond correct syntax, input must be validated for business-context correctness — that the value makes sense for the operation, the state, and the user requesting it.

**Vulnerability Pattern (Description):** A value is accepted because it is well-formed, but its meaning is never checked against business rules, so a syntactically valid start date after an end date, a negative transfer amount, or a quantity beyond stock is processed.

**Secure Pattern (Description):** After syntactic checks pass, the value is validated against the business rules of the operation — date ordering, value bounds, referential validity, and the current state of the entity.

[[C# PATTERNS]]
Vulnerable:
```
account.Balance -= amount;
```
Secure:
```
if (amount <= 0 || amount > account.Balance)
    return UnprocessableEntity("invalid amount");
account.Balance -= amount;
```

[[GENERIC PATTERNS]]
Vulnerable:
```
scheduleEvent(startDate, endDate)        # both are valid dates
```
Secure:
```
if startDate >= endDate: reject("start must precede end")
scheduleEvent(startDate, endDate)
```

[[JAVA PATTERNS]]
Vulnerable:
```
booking.setDates(start, end);
```
Secure:
```
if (!start.isBefore(end)) throw new UnprocessableEntity("bad date range");
booking.setDates(start, end);
```

[[JAVASCRIPT PATTERNS]]
Vulnerable:
```
order.quantity = req.body.quantity;
```
Secure:
```
if (req.body.quantity > product.stock)
    return res.status(422).send("exceeds available stock");
order.quantity = req.body.quantity;
```

[[PYTHON PATTERNS]]
Vulnerable:
```
account.balance -= transfer_amount
```
Secure:
```
if transfer_amount <= 0 or transfer_amount > account.balance:
    abort(422, "invalid transfer amount")
account.balance -= transfer_amount
```
embed_text:
Beyond correct syntax, input must be validated for business-context correctness — that the value makes sense for the operation, the state, and the user requesting it.
A value is accepted because it is well-formed, but its meaning is never checked against business rules, so a syntactically valid start date after an end date, a negative transfer amount, or a quantity beyond stock is processed.
After syntactic checks pass, the value is validated against the business rules of the operation — date ordering, value bounds, referential validity, and the current state of the entity.

document:
**Security Rule:** Structured input such as JSON or XML payloads must be validated against a strict schema that fixes the allowed fields, types, and constraints, and rejects unknown or extra properties.

**Vulnerability Pattern (Description):** A request body is deserialized straight onto a domain object or model with no schema, so unexpected or extra fields are bound, enabling mass-assignment of attributes the client should not control.

**Secure Pattern (Description):** The payload is parsed into a strict typed schema or data-transfer object that declares every permitted field and forbids unknown properties, and only the validated result is mapped to domain state.

[[C# PATTERNS]]
Vulnerable:
```
var user = JsonSerializer.Deserialize<User>(body);
```
Secure:
```
var dto = JsonSerializer.Deserialize<UserDto>(body);
Validator.ValidateObject(dto, new ValidationContext(dto), true);
```

[[GENERIC PATTERNS]]
Vulnerable:
```
user = deserialize(request.body)        # binds every field present
```
Secure:
```
dto = parseWithSchema(request.body, UserSchema)   # unknown fields rejected
user = mapAllowedFields(dto)
```

[[JAVA PATTERNS]]
Vulnerable:
```
User user = objectMapper.readValue(body, User.class);
```
Secure:
```
UserDto dto = objectMapper.readValue(body, UserDto.class);
Set<ConstraintViolation<UserDto>> v = validator.validate(dto);
```

[[JAVASCRIPT PATTERNS]]
Vulnerable:
```
const user = { ...req.body };
```
Secure:
```
const dto = userSchema.strict().parse(req.body);   // zod rejects extra keys
```

[[PYTHON PATTERNS]]
Vulnerable:
```
user = User(**request.get_json())
```
Secure:
```
dto = UserCreate.model_validate(request.get_json())   # pydantic, extra=forbid
user = User(name=dto.name, email=dto.email)
```
embed_text:
Structured input such as JSON or XML payloads must be validated against a strict schema that fixes the allowed fields, types, and constraints, and rejects unknown or extra properties.
A request body is deserialized straight onto a domain object or model with no schema, so unexpected or extra fields are bound, enabling mass-assignment of attributes the client should not control.
The payload is parsed into a strict typed schema or data-transfer object that declares every permitted field and forbids unknown properties, and only the validated result is mapped to domain state.

document:
**Security Rule:** Input validation must be treated as a defense-in-depth layer that complements, and never replaces, context-aware output encoding and parameterized queries at each sink.

**Vulnerability Pattern (Description):** Validation alone is relied on to stop injection, so a value that is valid for its business meaning, such as a name containing an apostrophe, still breaks the SQL or HTML context it is later placed into.

**Secure Pattern (Description):** Input is validated for correctness on entry and, independently, encoded or parameterized for the specific interpreter at every sink, so neither layer alone is the only protection.

[[C# PATTERNS]]
Vulnerable:
```
if (Valid(name)) cmd.CommandText = "SELECT * FROM u WHERE n='" + name + "'";
```
Secure:
```
if (Valid(name)) { cmd.CommandText = "SELECT * FROM u WHERE n=@n";
    cmd.Parameters.AddWithValue("@n", name); }
```

[[GENERIC PATTERNS]]
Vulnerable:
```
if isValidName(name): query("SELECT ... WHERE n = '" + name + "'")
```
Secure:
```
if isValidName(name): query("SELECT ... WHERE n = ?", [name])
```

[[JAVA PATTERNS]]
Vulnerable:
```
if (valid(name)) stmt.executeQuery("SELECT * FROM u WHERE n='" + name + "'");
```
Secure:
```
if (valid(name)) { var ps = conn.prepareStatement(
    "SELECT * FROM u WHERE n=?"); ps.setString(1, name); }
```

[[JAVASCRIPT PATTERNS]]
Vulnerable:
```
if (isValid(name)) el.innerHTML = name;
```
Secure:
```
if (isValid(name)) el.textContent = name;
```

[[PYTHON PATTERNS]]
Vulnerable:
```
if name.isalpha():
    cursor.execute("SELECT * FROM u WHERE name = '" + name + "'")
```
Secure:
```
if name.isalpha():
    cursor.execute("SELECT * FROM u WHERE name = %s", (name,))
```
embed_text:
Input validation must be treated as a defense-in-depth layer that complements, and never replaces, context-aware output encoding and parameterized queries at each sink.
Validation alone is relied on to stop injection, so a value that is valid for its business meaning, such as a name containing an apostrophe, still breaks the SQL or HTML context it is later placed into.
Input is validated for correctness on entry and, independently, encoded or parameterized for the specific interpreter at every sink, so neither layer alone is the only protection.
